title: Amanda Tan
text:
Hi, I’m Amanda! I’m an MSBA student at UC San Diego, graduating in June 2026.
I grew up in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia 🇲🇾 and made my way to San Diego to pursue a career at the intersection of data and brand storytelling. I’m passionate about using analytics to understand consumer behavior, drive marketing strategy, and build brands that people genuinely love.
My ultimate goal is to work as a Marketing Analyst for a luxury beauty or fashion house — think LVMH: Dior, Sephora, Fenty, Givenchy. I’m drawn to brands that blend creativity with precision, and I believe great marketing decisions start with great data.
Outside of work, you’ll find me testing new skincare routines, keeping up with runway season, and hunting for the best matcha latte in whatever city I’m in.
Currently open to: Full-time marketing analytics roles starting Summer/Fall 2026.

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
S-Mobile is a Singapore-based telecom provider facing a 2% monthly churn rate across its 1M+ customer base. The goal of this project was to build a predictive model to identify customers at high risk of churning, and then translate that model into actionable, financially-justified retention strategies.
This was a group project completed with teammates Damilare Shonuyi and Shalini Harwalkar.

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
S-Mobile is a Singapore-based telecom provider facing a 2% monthly churn rate across its 1M+ customer base. The goal of this project was to build a predictive model to identify customers at high risk of churning, and then translate that model into actionable, financially-justified retention strategies.
This was a group project completed with teammates Damilare Shonuyi and Shalini Harwalkar.

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
The Data
The dataset contained 69,000 customer records split into three samples:



Sample
Size
Churn Rate




Training
27,300
50% (balanced)


Test
11,700
50% (balanced)


Representative
30,000
2% (real-world)



Key variables included monthly revenue, minutes of use, overage minutes, roaming calls, dropped calls, handset age, customer care contacts, retention call history, and demographic/lifestyle indicators (region, occupation, car/motorcycle ownership, travel history).

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
Approach
1. Logistic Regression Model
We built and iteratively refined a logistic regression model to predict churn probability. Key predictors included overage minutes, revenue change, equipment age, and number of customer care calls. The model was evaluated on the test set before being applied to the representative (real-world) sample.
2. CLV-Based Intervention Analysis
Rather than stopping at predictions, we used Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) analysis to evaluate the financial impact of targeted retention interventions. We identified high-risk segments and modeled what would happen if churn rates were reduced by 20% through targeted discounts.

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
Key Results
Focusing on overage customers (those exceeding their plan minutes) as a high-churn, high-value segment:



Metric
Value




Avg. monthly revenue
SGD 75.65


CLV before intervention
SGD 552.62


CLV after intervention
SGD 651.13


CLV gain per customer
SGD 98.52


Cost per customer
SGD 20.87


Customers targeted (1M base)
521,867


Net benefit
SGD 40,519,171



The proposed intervention — a 10% overage discount — costs SGD 20.87 per customer against a breakeven threshold of SGD 98.52, making it a highly favorable strategy.

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
Tools & Methods

Python (Polars, pyrsm, NumPy)
Logistic Regression
Customer Lifetime Value modeling
Train/test/representative sample framework

title: S-Mobile: Predicting Customer Churn
text:
Takeaways
This project reinforced how predictive modeling only creates value when connected to business decisions and financial outcomes. Identifying who will churn is step one — knowing how much it’s worth to keep them is what drives action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Say you run a website with a newsletter sign-up form. You’ve got two versions of the prompt, the “call to action”, and you want to know which one actually gets people to subscribe:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

They seem basically the same. But “basically the same” isn’t a data-driven take. To actually figure this out, we run an A/B test: randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs, see who signs up, and compare.
The randomization matters a lot here. By randomly assigning visitors, we make sure any difference in sign-up rates is because of the CTA and not because of something else, like what time of day it is or what kind of person happens to be browsing.
This blog post walks through the statistics behind that setup, using simulation so we can actually see how everything works.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Say you run a website with a newsletter sign-up form. You’ve got two versions of the prompt, the “call to action”, and you want to know which one actually gets people to subscribe:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

They seem basically the same. But “basically the same” isn’t a data-driven take. To actually figure this out, we run an A/B test: randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs, see who signs up, and compare.
The randomization matters a lot here. By randomly assigning visitors, we make sure any difference in sign-up rates is because of the CTA and not because of something else, like what time of day it is or what kind of person happens to be browsing.
This blog post walks through the statistics behind that setup, using simulation so we can actually see how everything works.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or they don’t, so we model their outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution: with probability \(\pi\) they sign up (1), and with probability \(1 - \pi\) they don’t (0).
Since the two CTAs might perform differently, we let the sign-up probability differ between groups (\(\pi_A\) for group A and \(\pi_B\) for group B). The thing we actually care about is:
\[\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A wins. If \(\theta < 0\), CTA B wins. If \(\theta = 0\), they’re the same and we’ve done a lot of work for nothing.
Since we can’t observe \(\pi_A\) or \(\pi_B\) directly, we estimate \(\theta\) using the difference in sample proportions:
\[\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\]
Because each observation is just a 0 or 1, the sample mean is the proportion who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\). Simple and interpretable.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In real life we wouldn’t know \(\pi_A\) or \(\pi_B\) — that’s the whole point of running the test. But here we get to set them ourselves and see how well our estimator recovers the truth.
We’ll use \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\). We simulate 1,000 visitors per group.

np.random.seed(42)
pi_A, pi_B, n = 0.22, 0.18, 1000
data_A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
data_B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

print("Sample mean CTA A:", round(data_A.mean(), 4))
print("Sample mean CTA B:", round(data_B.mean(), 4))
print("Estimated difference:", round(data_A.mean() - data_B.mean(), 4))

Sample mean CTA A: 0.216
Sample mean CTA B: 0.178
Estimated difference: 0.038


Our estimate is pretty close to the true 0.04. Below, it is explained why that’s expected, how uncertain we should be about it, and what we can actually conclude from this.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) says that as you collect more data, your sample mean gets closer and closer to the true population mean. It’s the reason we trust \(\hat\theta\) to be close to \(\theta\) when our sample is large.
To show this, we compute the element-wise differences between the two groups and track the running average as observations pile up:

diffs   = data_A - data_B
cum_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)










At the start, the running average is all over the place — a few unlucky draws can throw it way off. But as the sample grows, the noise averages out and the estimate settles near 0.04. The chaos on the left side of the plot is the whole point: it’s showing us exactly why you can’t trust estimates from tiny samples. (Relevant foreshadowing for the peeking section.)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate on its own only tells us so much. We also want to know how much we should trust it, how precise is our estimate of \(\theta\)?
That’s where the bootstrap comes in. The idea: since we can’t re-run the experiment, we simulate the variability of our estimator by resampling from the data we already have.

Draw a resample of size 1,000 with replacement from each group
Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\)
Repeat 1,000 times
Take the standard deviation of those estimates, that’s the bootstrap standard error


boot_estimates = [
    np.random.choice(data_A, n, replace=True).mean() -
    np.random.choice(data_B, n, replace=True).mean()
    for _ in range(1000)
]
boot_se = np.std(boot_estimates)

pi_hat_A      = data_A.mean()
pi_hat_B      = data_B.mean()
analytical_se = np.sqrt(pi_hat_A*(1-pi_hat_A)/n + pi_hat_B*(1-pi_hat_B)/n)

theta_hat = pi_hat_A - pi_hat_B
ci_lower  = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper  = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

We can compare the bootstrap SE to the closed-form analytical formula:
\[SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}\]







Table 1: Bootstrap vs. Analytical Standard Error





Method
Standard Error




Bootstrap
0.017650


Analytical
0.017770








They come out basically the same, which is reassuring. The bootstrap does a ton of computation to get to the same answer the formula gives for free. But it’s good to know both agree.
Using the bootstrap SE, our 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) is:

print(f"95% CI: [{round(ci_lower, 4)}, {round(ci_upper, 4)}]")

95% CI: [0.0034, 0.0726]


The whole interval is above zero, which is already a good sign that CTA A is genuinely better.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is probably the most important result in intro stats. It says that no matter what the underlying distribution looks like, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as your sample size grows. That applies to \(\hat\theta\) too even though our raw data are just 0s and 1s.
To see it happen, we simulate the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) at four different sample sizes and plot the histograms:









At \(n = 25\) the histogram is choppy and kind of rough. By \(n = 100\) it’s starting to look bell-shaped, and at \(n = 500\) it’s smooth and nearly perfectly Normal, centered right on 0.04. This is why we can use Normal-based tests and confidence intervals in practice. Large samples give us Normality essentially for free, even when the data themselves are binary.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now we can formally test whether the two CTAs actually produce different sign-up rates, or whether what we’re seeing is just noise.
We set up two hypotheses:

\(H_0: \theta = 0\) - the CTAs perform the same
\(H_1: \theta \neq 0\) — one CTA is better

By the CLT, \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), its mean is zero. Dividing by the standard error gives a test statistic that follows a standard Normal under the null:
\[z = \frac{\hat\theta}{SE(\hat\theta)} \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\]
The p-value tells us how likely we’d be to see a result this extreme if \(H_0\) were actually true. Small p-value = surprising result under the null = evidence against \(H_0\).
Quick note: this is sometimes called a t-test. A true t-test assumes Normal data and an exact t-distribution for the test statistic. Our data are Bernoulli, so we’re using the CLT to justify approximate Normality. It’s more accurately a z-test. For large samples the two are basically identical.

z_stat  = theta_hat / analytical_se
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

print(f"Test statistic: {round(z_stat, 4)}")
print(f"P-value:        {round(p_value, 4)}")

Test statistic: 2.1388
P-value:        0.0325









Table 2: Hypothesis Test Results





Statistic
Value




θ̂ (estimated difference)
0.038000


SE(θ̂)
0.017800


z-statistic
2.138800


p-value
0.032500








The p-value is below 0.05, so we reject \(H_0\). There’s statistically significant evidence that the two CTAs produce different sign-up rates. Since we set the true values ourselves, we know this is the right call — CTA A wins.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
This is something that initially sounds like a coincidence but is actually pretty useful: the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression.
Stack both groups into one dataset, with an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 = signed up, 0 = didn’t) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 = saw CTA A, 0 = saw CTA B). Fit:
\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i\]
What the coefficients mean:

\(\beta_0\) = expected outcome when \(D_i = 0\) = the B group sign-up rate, \(\hat\pi_B\)
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) = expected outcome when \(D_i = 1\) = the A group sign-up rate, \(\hat\pi_A\)
So \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\) — and \(\hat\beta_1\) is the same number as \(\hat\theta\)


import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

reg_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Y": np.concatenate([data_A, data_B]),
    "D": np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])
})

model = smf.ols("Y ~ D", data=reg_data).fit()








Table 3: Regression Output





 
Estimate
Std. Error
t-statistic
p-value




D (CTA A indicator)
0.038000
0.017800
2.137700
0.032700








The estimate and p-value match what we got from the manual test (the SE is slightly different because OLS uses a pooled variance, while our earlier formula kept the groups separate).
The regression framework generalizes naturally to more complex designs — add covariates to control for confounders, indicators for additional treatment arms, or interaction terms to test whether the CTA effect varies across subgroups. The two-sample t-test is just the simplest special case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
So far, we’ve run a single test on a complete dataset. But in practice, it’s tempting to check results early — and if things look significant, call it there.
Instead of waiting for all 1,000 visitors, you want to check results after every 100 — and if any of those 10 interim tests hits \(p < 0.05\), you can call it and move on.
This is called peeking, and it quietly breaks everything.
The issue: a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% false positive rate. But every additional peek is another chance to get a false positive. Run 10 tests on the same accumulating data and the probability of at least one incorrect rejection is way above 5%.
Let’s see how bad it actually gets. We simulate 10,000 experiments where \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (no true difference) and peek every 100 observations:

np.random.seed(42)
false_pos = 0
peek_at   = range(100, 1001, 100)

for _ in range(10000):
    A = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)
    for k in peek_at:
        a, b = A[:k], B[:k]
        se = np.sqrt(a.mean()*(1-a.mean())/k + b.mean()*(1-b.mean())/k)
        if se == 0: continue
        z = (a.mean() - b.mean()) / se
        if 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z))) < 0.05:
            false_pos += 1
            break

empirical_fpr = false_pos / 10000
print("Empirical false positive rate:", round(empirical_fpr, 4))

Empirical false positive rate: 0.1864









Table 4: The Cost of Peeking — H₀: πA = πB = 0.20





Approach
False Positive Rate




Single test (no peeking)
5.0%


10 peeks (every 100 obs)
18.6%








The false positive rate with peeking ends up around 3 to 4 times the nominal 5%. That means roughly 1 in 3 experiments where there’s no real difference would produce a “significant” result.
The fix isn’t to never look at data early. It’s to use methods built for sequential testing — like alpha spending or sequential probability ratio tests — that account for the multiple looks. But that’s a topic for another post.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: Toast: A Case Study
text:
Key Findings
Sleep Analysis: Toast logs approximately 14–16 hours of sleep per day, consistently outperforming industry benchmarks for household cats.
Zoomies Frequency: Peaks observed between 2–4am. Causation unknown.
Adoption Impact: Studies show black cats are adopted at lower rates than other cats. Toast is a clear outlier and an excellent data point in favor of black cats.

title: Toast: A Case Study
text:
Conclusion
Toast represents a high-value, low-maintenance asset with strong long-term growth potential. His upcoming 3rd birthday marks a significant milestone and warrants further observation.

Data collected via direct observation. Sample size: 1 cat.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
In 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour. Pennsylvania kept its minimum wage the same. David Card and Alan Krueger saw this as a natural experiment: if you want to know whether a minimum wage increase kills jobs, here’s a real-world test case with a ready-made control group right next door.
The standard prediction from economics textbooks is pretty intuitive — if you force employers to pay more, they’ll hire fewer people. Card and Krueger set out to test that assumption by surveying 410 fast-food restaurants in NJ and eastern PA before and after the NJ increase, then comparing employment trends across the two states.
Their finding was controversial: employment in NJ didn’t fall. If anything, it went up slightly relative to PA. This post replicates their key results using the original survey data.
The method they use is called difference-in-differences (DiD). The idea is to compare how much employment changed in NJ (treated) versus how much it changed in PA (control) over the same period. By differencing out the time trend, we’re left with just the effect of the policy:
\[\hat\delta = (\bar{E}_{NJ,\text{after}} - \bar{E}_{NJ,\text{before}}) - (\bar{E}_{PA,\text{after}} - \bar{E}_{PA,\text{before}})\]
This works as long as NJ and PA would have followed similar employment trends in the absence of the policy — the parallel trends assumption.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
In 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour. Pennsylvania kept its minimum wage the same. David Card and Alan Krueger saw this as a natural experiment: if you want to know whether a minimum wage increase kills jobs, here’s a real-world test case with a ready-made control group right next door.
The standard prediction from economics textbooks is pretty intuitive — if you force employers to pay more, they’ll hire fewer people. Card and Krueger set out to test that assumption by surveying 410 fast-food restaurants in NJ and eastern PA before and after the NJ increase, then comparing employment trends across the two states.
Their finding was controversial: employment in NJ didn’t fall. If anything, it went up slightly relative to PA. This post replicates their key results using the original survey data.
The method they use is called difference-in-differences (DiD). The idea is to compare how much employment changed in NJ (treated) versus how much it changed in PA (control) over the same period. By differencing out the time trend, we’re left with just the effect of the policy:
\[\hat\delta = (\bar{E}_{NJ,\text{after}} - \bar{E}_{NJ,\text{before}}) - (\bar{E}_{PA,\text{after}} - \bar{E}_{PA,\text{before}})\]
This works as long as NJ and PA would have followed similar employment trends in the absence of the policy — the parallel trends assumption.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Main Analysis

Table 2: Summary Statistics
Before jumping into the DiD, the authors first check that NJ and PA stores looked similar before the minimum wage increase — otherwise the comparison isn’t very credible. Table 2 reports means of key variables by state in both waves.









PA
NJ
Difference (NJ - PA)




FTE Employment (Wave 1)
23.33
20.44
-2.89


FTE Employment (Wave 2)
21.17
21.03
-0.14


Starting Wage (Wave 1)
4.63
4.61
-0.02


Starting Wage (Wave 2)
4.62
5.08
0.46


Price of Full Meal (Wave 1)
3.03
3.35
0.32


Hours Open/Day (Wave 1)
14.53
14.42
-0.11







A few things stand out. In Wave 1, NJ and PA stores look pretty similar — starting wages, hours open, and meal prices are close. The one notable pre-existing difference is that PA stores were slightly larger on average in terms of FTE employment. By Wave 2, NJ wages jumped sharply (as expected after the minimum wage hike) while PA wages stayed flat.


Table 3: The Difference-in-Differences
This is the core of the paper. Rows 1–3 show FTE employment before, after, and the change — separately for PA (column i), NJ (column ii), and the difference between them (column iii).

t3 = df.groupby('STATE')[['FTE','FTE2','dFTE']].mean()
t3.index = ['PA','NJ']
t3 = t3.T
t3['NJ - PA'] = t3['NJ'] - t3['PA']
t3.index = ['FTE before (Wave 1)', 'FTE after (Wave 2)', 'Change in FTE']
t3.round(2)








PA
NJ
NJ - PA




FTE before (Wave 1)
23.33
20.44
-2.89


FTE after (Wave 2)
21.17
21.03
-0.14


Change in FTE
-2.28
0.47
2.75







The key number is in the bottom-right: the difference-in-differences estimate. NJ employment went up while PA employment went down, leaving a relative gain of about 2.76 FTE employees in NJ — or roughly 13% — after the minimum wage increase. This is the opposite of what the textbook predicts.


Table 4: Regression Version
The DiD can also be estimated as a simple regression. If we define \(\Delta E_i\) as the change in FTE employment at store \(i\) and \(NJ_i\) as an indicator for being in New Jersey, then:
\[\Delta E_i = \alpha + \delta \cdot NJ_i + \varepsilon_i\]
The coefficient \(\delta\) is numerically the same as the DiD estimate above. The regression framework just lets us add controls and compute standard errors cleanly.

reg_df = df[['dFTE','STATE','WAGE_ST']].dropna()
model  = smf.ols('dFTE ~ STATE', data=reg_df).fit()

result = model.summary2().tables[1].loc[['STATE']]
result.index = ['NJ dummy']
result.columns = ['Estimate','Std. Error','t-statistic','p-value','CI Lower','CI Upper']
result[['Estimate','Std. Error','t-statistic','p-value']].round(2)








Estimate
Std. Error
t-statistic
p-value




NJ dummy
2.36
1.15
2.06
0.04







The NJ coefficient comes out to about 2.33 — consistent with Table 3, with the small difference due to the regression dropping stores that are missing wage data. The standard error is around 1.19, giving a t-statistic just under 2. The effect is statistically significant at the 5% level.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
section: Something Additional: Visualizing the Parallel Trends
text:
Something Additional: Visualizing the Parallel Trends
One assumption that’s baked into every DiD is parallel trends — the idea that NJ and PA would have had similar employment trajectories if the minimum wage hadn’t changed. We can’t fully test this with only two time periods, but we can visualize what actually happened to get a sense of whether the design is credible.









The dashed line shows what NJ employment would have looked like if it had followed the same trend as PA — the counterfactual. The gap between the dashed line and the actual NJ outcome in Wave 2 is the DiD estimate. NJ employment held up while PA’s declined, producing a positive relative effect.
This plot also gives us some informal reassurance about parallel trends: both states were moving in the same direction coming into the study (driven by the broader recession in the Mid-Atlantic states), which supports the assumption that they would have continued along similar paths without the policy change.

Card and Krueger’s finding remains one of the most cited and debated results in labor economics. The intuition — that a minimum wage increase leads to job losses — seems airtight in theory, but this paper showed that real labor markets don’t always behave like textbook models. Their work helped shift the conversation toward more nuanced views of how wages and employment interact, and diff-in-diff became a go-to tool for evaluating policy changes across many fields.
